blocks.statement: I can state conservation of momentum (Σp_before = Σp_after) and its condition (no net external force), and I can use it to solve recoil/explosion problems.

blocks.whatWeKnow: When DART hit Dimorphos, total momentum BEFORE = total momentum AFTER. The asteroid sped up; the spacecraft slowed (and was destroyed).
blocks.connection: Today's rule is the BIG one of the unit. The next eight days are all special cases of it.

blocks.markdown:
## The big rule

**Σp_before = Σp_after** — total momentum of a system stays constant, **provided no net EXTERNAL force acts**.

Internal forces don't count. Bullet pushes rifle, rifle pushes bullet — those are internal to the bullet+rifle system, and they cancel (Newton's 3rd Law, Unit 1). Only an outside push — friction, gravity pulling the system sideways, a wall — can change the total.

For recoil/explosion problems the recipe is short: the system starts at rest, so **Σp = 0 before and 0 after**. Whatever momentum one piece carries east, the other piece must carry west.

terms.definition: If the NET external force on a system is zero, the TOTAL momentum of the system stays constant: Σp_before = Σp_after. A rifle at rest fires a bullet east; the rifle recoils west; total p stays zero.
terms.cognate: Sp. conservación del momento · Pt. conservação do momento · HC konsèvasyon mouvman

terms.definition: When an object at rest splits into two pieces (or expels mass), the pieces move in opposite directions so total p stays the same. The smaller mass moves FASTER: m₁v₁ = m₂v₂ in size, opposite in sign.
terms.cognate: Sp. retroceso · Pt. recuo · HC rekil

blocks.title: The condition is NO NET EXTERNAL FORCE — not 'no forces'
blocks.markdown: Internal forces (between bullet and rifle, between two exploding pieces) are fine — they cancel in pairs. Only EXTERNAL forces (friction, a wall, gravity acting unevenly) can change the system's total p.

blocks.title: Watch recoil happen
blocks.markdown: Run the third-law carts simulation below: two carts at rest push apart. Watch the lighter cart leave faster — exactly what 0 = m₁v₁ + m₂v₂ demands.

blocks.prompt: A 70-kg ice skater (initially at rest) throws a 5-kg ball east at 8 m/s. What is the skater's recoil velocity?
blocks.given: m_skater = 70 kg · m_ball = 5 kg · v_ball = +8 m/s east · both at rest before
blocks.answer: Skater drifts WEST at about 0.57 m/s. (Small, because she's 14× heavier than the ball.)

blocks.prompt: A 0.5-kg firework at REST explodes into two pieces: a 0.3-kg chunk goes east at 12 m/s. The other piece (0.2 kg) goes — what direction and how fast?
blocks.givenHint: Total p before = 0. Piece 1: 0.3 kg at +12 m/s. Piece 2: 0.2 kg at v = ?
blocks.equationHint: 0 = m₁v₁ + m₂v₂ — solve for v₂ and read its sign as a direction.

blocks.prompt: Check your firework answer, then complete the frame about WHY total momentum stayed the same.
blocks.frame: The 0.2-kg piece goes ___ at ___ m/s. Total momentum stays the same because the only forces during the explosion are ___ (between the pieces), which means no NET ___ force.

blocks.title: Read & practice
blocks.markdown: Open the reader and read **8.3–8.4** (bouncing makes impulses bigger; the law of conservation of momentum and cannon recoil). Answer the questions beside the reading as you go.
